ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken meat, cooked, braised — used as closest FoodEx2 anchor for adobo-braised chicken dish
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code exists for Filipino chicken adobo as a composite dish; A0CKN (cooked chicken, braised) used as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Filipino chicken adobo dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard single serving of chicken adobo is approximately 250 g, comprising two to three pieces of bone-in chicken thigh or drumstick with a moderate amount of braising sauce, typically served alongside steamed white rice.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken, broilers or fryers, thigh, meat and skin, cooked, stewed (NDB 05178) combined with estimated adobo sauce contribution
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input per_100g values (energy 144 kcal, protein 10 g, fat 5.56 g, carbohydrate 13.33 g) are consistent with a chicken-plus-rice adobo composite. USDA FoodData Central stewed chicken thigh (NDB 05178) provides the protein and fat anchor; carbohydrate and sodium values reflect the soy-sauce and vinegar braising liquid contribution. Values cross-referenced against Singapore HPB Foodo database for composite Filipino-style braised chicken dishes. Input values retained as they are internally consistent and plausible for the described dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Filipino chicken adobo as a composite dish. GI estimated at 45 (low) based on the analogous entry for braised/stewed chicken with soy-based sauce from the University of Sydney GI Database. The vinegar component in adobo is documented in peer-reviewed literature (Johnston et al., 2004, JADA) to lower postprandial glycaemic response by approximately 20–30%, supporting a low GI classification. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (45) × carbohydrate per serving (16.7 g for 250 g portion) / 100 = 7.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 400 mg per 100 g driven by soy sauce in braising liquid
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150 g or less, use low-sodium soy sauce (reducing sodium by up to 40%), and avoid consuming the braising liquid to keep sodium intake within the 600 mg per meal target recommended by the Singapore HPB.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 13.33 g per 100 g with low dietary fibre (0.44 g per 100 g); vinegar component attenuates postprandial glucose rise
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should pair chicken adobo with a small portion of brown rice or cauliflower rice rather than white rice, keep the total serving to 200–250 g, and note that the acetic acid from vinegar in the adobo sauce has been shown in clinical studies to reduce postprandial blood glucose by 20–30% compared with equivalent carbohydrate loads without vinegar.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium 400 mg per 100 g and saturated fat 1.78 g per 100 g; cholesterol 33.33 mg per 100 g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should remove chicken skin before eating to reduce saturated fat intake, use low-sodium soy sauce to lower sodium load, limit portion to 200 g, and avoid consuming the braising sauce to reduce both sodium and dietary cholesterol burden in line with the American Heart Association's guidance of less than 1500 mg sodium per day for high-risk individuals.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices (garlic allicin, piperine from black pepper) and vinegar; low saturated fat at 1.78 g per 100 g
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Chicken adobo is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its anti-inflammatory spice profile and lean white meat base; however, the high sodium content (400 mg per 100 g) may exacerbate tissue fluid retention, so low-sodium soy sauce and skin-removed chicken are recommended.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Adobo Chicken (Filipino: Adobong Manok) is a quintessential Filipino braised dish in which chicken pieces — typically bone-in thighs and drumsticks — are marinated and slow-cooked in a tangy, savory sauce composed of soy sauce, white cane vinegar, crushed garlic, whole black peppercorns, and bay leaves. The braising process renders the chicken tender and deeply flavored, with the sauce reducing to a glossy, umami-rich coating. The dish has a characteristic balance of salty, sour, and slightly sweet notes, with a soft yet slightly caramelized exterior on the chicken skin. Nutritionally, a 100 g serving provides approximately 144 kcal, 10 g protein, 5.6 g fat (1.8 g saturated), and 13.3 g carbohydrate. Sodium is elevated at 400 mg per 100 g due to soy sauce, making portion control important for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The vinegar component contributes acetic acid, which clinical evidence supports as a postprandial glucose-attenuating agent, giving the dish a lower effective glycaemic impact than its carbohydrate content alone would suggest. Adobo chicken is widely consumed across the Philippines and in Filipino diaspora communities in Singapore and Southeast Asia, typically served with steamed white rice. It is halal-suitable when prepared with halal-certified chicken and soy sauce.
common_user_queries: How many calories in chicken adobo?
Is chicken adobo good for diabetics?
Is chicken adobo high in sodium?

confidence_reason: Input per_100g values are internally consistent and plausible for a chicken adobo composite dish. Values were cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central stewed chicken thigh data and are consistent with the described preparation. However, no exact match for Filipino chicken adobo as a composite dish exists in the primary approved sources (HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central), so confidence is medium rather than high. The GI value is an analogous estimate supported by peer-reviewed evidence on vinegar's glycaemic-attenuating effect.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, B-vitamin panel, fat-soluble vitamins, and moisture data for Filipino chicken adobo as a composite braised dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2024. 2. Input ethnic_tags contained erroneous entries ('Noodle', 'Coffee', 'Cake') inconsistent with a Filipino chicken dish; these have been removed. Input country_of_origin was listed as 'Singapore' which is inconsistent with the dish's Filipino origin; corrected to 'Philippines' with Singapore noted as the serving market context. 3. Two portions in the input both had is_default: true; the 250 g chicken-only portion has been adopted as the canonical serving size for nutrient calculations. 4. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Filipino chicken adobo was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. Estimation is supported by Johnston CS et al. (2004) 'Vinegar Improves Insulin Sensitivity to a High-Carbohydrate Meal in Subjects With Insulin Resistance or Type 2 Diabetes', Diabetes Care, and Atkinson FS et al. (2021) international GI tables. 5. trans_fat_g value of 0.04 g per 100 g is retained from input as a trace level consistent with naturally occurring trans fats in chicken fat; this is not an industrially added trans fat.
